title: How Basel III Changes Where Capital Sits: Nonbank Subsidiaries as Equity Reservoirs - Liberty Street Economics
gist: When Basel III's binding capital minimums took effect for U.S. banks in January 2015, organizationally complex bank holding companies met tighter bank-level requirements not by raising costly external equity but by relocating existing equity out of their equity-rich nonbank subsidiaries and into their depository banks. Consolidated capital was unchanged; capital simply moved to where it was regulated.

themes.theme: Core mechanism: relocate capital rather than raise it

insights.claim: A bank holding company whose depository subsidiary fell short of Basel III's 2015 minimums could either raise costly fresh external equity or, if it owned equity-rich nonbank affiliates, simply move capital from one subsidiary to another—satisfying the regulator while leaving consolidated equity unchanged.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Regulatory capital ratios at the bank level can strengthen without any new capital entering the consolidated firm, meaning headline bank capitalization can overstate true incremental loss-absorbing resources at complex BHCs.
insights.quote: The second route satisfies the regulator, avoids issuance costs, and leaves consolidated equity exactly where it was.
insights.locator: When Basel III's binding capital minimums

insights.claim: Organizationally complex bank holding companies responded to higher post-2015 capital requirements precisely by reallocating internal capital rather than tapping external markets.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The effectiveness of capital regulation depends on organizational structure; firms with internal capital markets can meet rules mechanically, a channel supervisors and equity analysts should map before trusting bank-level ratios.
insights.locator: In this second post of our series

insights.claim: The authors instrument for organizational complexity using historical variation in interstate banking deregulation, since BHCs headquartered in states that deregulated earlier had more time to build complex multi-subsidiary organizations before restrictions were nationally harmonized by the 1994 Riegle-Neal Act.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The pre-1994 deregulation timing is plausibly unrelated to 2015 Basel III implementation, giving the causal claim credibility that a simple cross-section of complex versus simple banks would lack.
insights.locator: To identify differences in organizational complexity

insights.claim: Banks in BHCs headquartered in states that deregulated one year earlier accumulate about three-quarters of a percentage point more excess capital after 2015, which across the roughly seven-year early-to-late deregulator gap translates to about 5 percentage points more excess capital at the bank level, while the consolidated BHC-level estimate is essentially zero.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: A large bank-level effect paired with a null consolidated effect is the smoking gun that capital was moved internally rather than raised externally—directly quantifying how much bank ratios can be inflated by structure.
insights.locator: Using this variation, we find that banks

insights.claim: BHCs with greater deregulation exposure significantly reduced external equity issuance after 2015, drawing on internal capital instead of tapping outside investors to meet tighter requirements.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Tighter bank capital rules did not translate into more equity issuance at structurally complex firms, weakening the dilution/capital-raising signal investors might expect from regulation and confirming the internal-reallocation story on the issuance margin directly.
insights.quote: BHCs with greater deregulation exposure significantly reduce external equity issuance after 2015.
insights.locator: But we can also see the issuance margin directly

insights.claim: After 2015, parent equity investments in bank subsidiaries rose by 2.28 percentage points of total BHC equity per year of deregulation exposure while investments in nonbank subsidiaries fell by a nearly identical 2.64 percentage points, with total parent investment across all subsidiaries unchanged.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The parent does not inject new capital—it changes where existing capital sits, so a rising bank capital ratio at these firms is offset one-for-one by a draining nonbank side.
insights.quote: The parent does not inject more capital into the organization; it changes where existing capital sits within it.
insights.locator: Step 1: The parent shifts equity toward banks

insights.claim: Following Basel III, dividends flowing from nonbank subsidiaries to the parent increased sharply (a statistically significant 0.0947 coefficient) while dividends from bank subsidiaries showed essentially no change, meaning the parent drew resources from the less-regulated side while leaving the regulated bank side untouched.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Nonbank affiliates function as the funding source for bank recapitalization; monitoring intra-firm dividend flows reveals capital migration invisible on the consolidated statement.
insights.quote: The parent effectively draws resources from the less-regulated side of the organization while leaving the regulated bank side largely untouched.
insights.locator: Step 2: The nonbanks supply the cash

insights.claim: Among BHCs with nonbank subsidiaries, banks increasingly retained earnings rather than distributing them upstream after 2015, while banks without access to nonbank affiliates showed no comparable change.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Earnings retention plus parent injections together build the bank capital buffer, so the availability of a nonbank affiliate is the differentiating factor in how banks respond to capital rules.

insights.claim: Nonbank subsidiaries act as 'equity reservoirs' because they hold a disproportionately large share of organizational equity relative to their assets, with the size of the reservoir captured by an equity multiplier equal to the nonbank share of consolidated equity relative to its share of assets.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The equity multiplier is a screenable metric for identifying which BHCs have the most redeployable internal capital and thus the greatest capacity to meet regulation without external raises.
insights.locator: The first post in this series introduced

insights.claim: Each additional unit of a BHC's 2013 equity multiplier is associated with a 1.71 percentage point larger post-2015 increase in the share of consolidated equity invested in banks and a 1.12 percentage point larger decline in the share invested in nonbanks.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The response to Basel III scales with reservoir size, confirming that redeployable nonbank equity—not organizational complexity alone—drives how a bank meets capital requirements.
insights.locator: Each additional unit of the 2013 equity multiplier

insights.claim: It is the availability of redeployable nonbank equity, not organizational complexity by itself, that determines how a BHC responds to tighter capital requirements.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Complexity is only a proxy; the sharper predictor of regulatory-arbitrage capacity is whether a firm holds equity-rich nonbank affiliates to draw on.
insights.quote: The availability of redeployable nonbank equity, not organizational complexity alone, determines how a BHC responds to tighter capital requirements.
insights.locator: The chart below shows the same relationship

themes.theme: Risk: where it sits versus how much

insights.claim: On the consolidated balance sheet, returns on assets and equity rose after Basel III while charge-offs fell with no shift toward riskier assets, so BHCs are not taking more risk in aggregate—they are reallocating capital across subsidiaries in response to differences in how those subsidiaries are regulated.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Aggregate risk metrics can look benign even as the internal distribution of risk shifts, so consolidated-level supervision may miss where fragility is migrating.
insights.quote: BHCs are not taking more risk in aggregate.

insights.claim: The same reallocation that strengthens banks weakens nonbank affiliates, shifts their activity toward riskier forms of lending, and creates a channel through which distress on the nonbank side can spill back onto the bank itself.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Draining nonbank equity to bolster banks builds a hidden contagion channel—the strengthened bank may be exposed to the very affiliate it was capitalized from, a fragility invisible on consolidated ratios.
insights.quote: the same reallocation that strengthens banks weakens nonbank affiliates, shifts their activity toward riskier forms of lending, and creates a channel through which distress on the nonbank side of the organization can spill back onto the bank itself
insights.locator: In the final post of this series
